audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua west-Jordan allotment introduction and Caleb-Hebron claim, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Eleazar/Joshua/tribal-head allocation, lot procedure, Joseph's two tribes, Levite cities, Caleb's Kadesh memory, forty-five-year endurance, age and strength, Anakim/fortified-city claim, blessing, and Hebron/Kiriath-arba tradition.

verses.restored: These are inheritances children of Israel received in land of Canaan, which Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and heads of fathers' houses of tribes of children of Israel caused them to inherit.
By lot was their inheritance, as YHWH commanded by hand of Moses, for nine tribes and half tribe.
For Moses had given inheritance of two tribes and half tribe across Jordan, but to Levites he gave no inheritance in their midst.
For sons of Joseph became two tribes, Manasseh and Ephraim; and they gave no portion in land to Levites except cities to dwell in, with pasturelands for their livestock and possessions.
As YHWH commanded Moses, so children of Israel did, and they divided land.
Sons of Judah drew near to Joshua at Gilgal, and Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite said to him, You know word YHWH spoke to Moses man of Divine concerning me and concerning you at Kadesh-barnea.
Forty years old was I when Moses servant of YHWH sent me from Kadesh-barnea to spy out land, and I returned word to him as it was with my heart.
My brothers who went up with me caused heart of people to melt, but I fully followed after YHWH my Divine.
Moses swore on that day, saying, Surely land upon which your foot trod shall be for you as inheritance and for your sons forever, because you fully followed after YHWH my Divine.
Now look, YHWH has kept me alive, as he spoke, these forty-five years since YHWH spoke this word to Moses while Israel walked in wilderness; and now look, today I am eighty-five years old.
Still today I am strong as on day Moses sent me; as my strength then, so my strength now, for war and for going out and coming in.

Now give me this hill country of which YHWH spoke on that day, for you heard on that day that Anakim were there and cities great and fortified. Perhaps YHWH will be with me, and I shall dispossess them, as YHWH spoke.
Joshua blessed him and gave Hebron to Caleb son of Jephunneh as inheritance.
Therefore Hebron became inheritance of Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite to this day, because he fully followed after YHWH Divine of Israel.
Name of Hebron formerly was Kiriath-arba; Arba was a great man among Anakim. And land rested from war.

translation_notes: Western allocation is shared among priestly, military-civic, and tribal leadership rather than controlled by Joshua alone.
Lot allocation expresses Divine ordering in the ancient narrative but is not an acceptable modern method for overriding inhabitants, land rights, or evidence.
Levites receive cities and pasturelands rather than a continuous tribal territory, requiring communal support without granting superior human status.
Caleb is identified as a Kenizzite within Judah, preserving a complex identity that resists simplistic ethnic purity narratives.
His memory emphasizes truthful reporting, long endurance, and a promise carried for forty-five years.
Caleb's vigor at eighty-five is celebrated as his individual experience. It must not shame elders who are frail, disabled, ill, or unable to work or fight.
His Hebron claim still entails dispossessing Anakim from fortified cities, so personal faithfulness cannot erase the violence experienced by current inhabitants.
The repeated 'land rested from war' formula remains a victor's summary and must be read beside unresolved inhabitants and future conflict.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 14 to justify conquest, land seizure by lot, ethnic-purity claims, ageism, ableism, or the idea that faithful elders must remain physically powerful. Center shared governance, long memory, truthful witness, honored aging in all bodily conditions, and promises interpreted without erasing existing communities.
